extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a serial industrial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes. The first axis is driven by a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor, followed by a gear transmission that includes a cycloidal gear; the remaining axes use similar motor‑gear configurations. The robot is designed for high‑precision machining and other complex manufacturing tasks, operating in a cyber‑physical production environment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides continuous, multi‑physics‑aware identification of joint friction and related wear, enabling real‑time monitoring of joint health, predictive maintenance, and optimization of motion accuracy. It streams live sensor data to a dashboard for visualization, supports prediction of future degradation, and offers control‑oriented recommendations to improve the robot’s positioning accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for semantic data exchange, SysML models for the robot’s structural and functional architecture, MATLAB/Simulink models for multi‑physics simulation, MQTT for real‑time sensor data streaming, and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl CNC controller for actuation. The digital shadow reference model and processing components defined in the DS framework provide traceability and data‑calculation logic.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system is orchestrated according to the Digital Shadow Reference Model. It comprises a data‑acquisition layer that collects raw data traces, a processing component that consumes these traces using engineering, simulation or data‑calculation models, and a storage layer that holds the resulting computed traces. Relationships such as the ‘originates‑from’ association maintain traceability across components. The concrete digital shadow model is a conforming realization of the reference model, mapping its abstract concepts to concrete classes and associations, thus defining the integration of acquisition, processing, and representation components within a unified architecture.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin employs a hybrid model stack. Physics‑based (white‑box) models are realized as Simulink dynamic simulations of robot joint friction and analytical friction equations. Complementary data‑driven (black‑box) components consist of neural‑network models that are trained on streamed sensor data. Input data traces originate from robot sensors capturing operating conditions, loads, temperature and payload; they are ingested by a Processing component that consumes these traces, runs them through the simulation or data‑calculation models and emits output traces that are persisted in a ThingsBoard instance. Model relationships are explicitly represented through the ‘originates‑from’ association, enabling transitive traceability from output traces back to the generating models and input traces. The combined models are used for real‑time friction compensation, predictive maintenance and for updating model parameters or retraining neural networks as new data become available.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Each drive houses two position encoders. The primary encoder is mounted directly on the PMSM shaft, delivering high‑resolution motor‑side angle measurements; the secondary encoder is located after the gearbox, providing joint‑side position data. The encoders generate time‑series data streams that are sampled at the controller’s cycle time and transmitted to the digital twin via the robot’s communication bus. Additional sensor channels (e.g., temperature, current) are also collected as time‑series and stored as data traces in the digital shadow.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is required to meet the real‑time constraints of the robot control loop; therefore the computational latency (dead time) of the simulation and neural‑network inference must be bounded. Validation is performed by traceability of each data trace to its originating processing component and input sources, as defined by the ‘originates‑from’ association. Verification of model transformations (e.g., SysML to Simulink) is emphasized to ensure correctness of the white‑box models. Uncertainty sources such as sensor noise, temperature variation and wear are mitigated by continuous model adaptation: friction model parameters are updated online and neural networks are retrained with the latest data. Quality assurance includes systematic verification of transformations and traceability checks, as highlighted in the digital‑shadow reference model.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The system implements an automated one‑way data flow from the physical asset to the digital representation, as defined for a digital shadow. Sensor measurements are collected as ‘data traces’, which are streamed from the physical devices to the digital side. The streaming is realized using the MQTT publish/subscribe protocol (e.g., via the Mosquitto broker) as indicated by the reference to the MQTT implementation. Incoming MQTT messages containing time‑series sensor data are consumed by a Processing component that applies data‑calculation or simulation models, producing higher‑level abstraction traces. The Processing component records the ‘originates‑from’ association, preserving traceability to the originating physical source. No explicit transmission frequency or event‑trigger conditions are documented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between the physical sensors and the digital shadow relies on the MQTT protocol (client‑broker model) as referenced by the Mosquitto server/client implementation. MQTT operates over TCP/IP, providing a publish/subscribe mechanism for the time‑series sensor streams. The documents do not specify the network topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security mechanisms beyond the use of MQTT.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin is developed following Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) where the system description is created in successive abstraction levels: requirements model, functional architecture model, logical architecture model, and product/technical layer model. These stages correspond to development phases and are managed by mapping each phase to a concrete representation (e.g., SysML requirement diagrams, SysML internal block diagrams for functional and logical architecture, and detailed engineering models for the product layer). Transition between phases is handled by traceability links defined in the reference model and by the ‘originates‑from’ association that connects data traces to the processing components that generated them. Lifecycle governance is implied by the use of reference models with a defined conformance relation that identifies which concrete models belong to the DT at each stage.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: DT creation follows established MBSE methods such as RFLP (Requirements‑Functions‑Logics‑Physics), OOSEM, SPES and the SMArDT approach. System structure is modelled with architecture description languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML internal‑block diagrams) and, where supported, behavior is added via MontiArcAutomaton or SysML state‑machines (v1.7). The models may be coupled to simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) or to executable code (C++, Java) for verification. Quality assurance relies on the ability of the ADLs to embed behavioral specifications that can be simulated or tested; the concretisation process enforces a conformance relation to the Digital‑Shadow Reference Model, providing traceability of model changes. Evolution management is achieved by maintaining the “originates‑from” links for data traces and by updating the concrete‑to‑reference mappings when models are refined.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is realised by a processing component that consumes input data traces, applies either data‑calculation models or simulation models, and emits new output traces. The computation logic is encoded in the selected model (e.g., a Simulink simulation or a discrete‑event calculation) and the resulting trace inherits provenance information through the ‘originates‑from’ association, enabling fine‑grained traceability back to the original physical measurements and to the processing component that produced it. These output traces constitute the analytical basis for downstream insights (e.g., performance indicators), while the explicit traceability permits users to assess the lineage of a recommendation.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The DT uses established modelling standards: SysML (requirements, block diagrams, internal block diagrams), AADL (architecture analysis), ACME (component‑connector ADL), and MontiArc‑Automaton for behaviour specifications. Conformance to the Digital Shadow Reference Model is defined by a precise conformance relation that maps concrete classes and associations to the reference class diagram, thereby enforcing interoperability between models. The approach also aligns with the MBSE practices and with the standards adopted in the cited works on Model‑Driven Engineering for Digital Twins.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_

This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital

and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the

more accurately than traditional static models.

integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial

behavior can significantly impact positioning accuracy and

to automatically update model parameters or retrain neural

This results in more robust and reliable friction compensation
strategies, reducing trajectory errors and enhancing the overall
performance of robotic systems. Moreover, the integration of
digital shadows into the robot’s control architecture supports
predictive maintenance and early fault detection, as anomalies
in friction behavior can be detected and addressed proactively.

a static, one-time process into a dynamic, continuously improving capability that drives higher accuracy and reliability
in industrial robotics.

Based on this paper, a digital twin of the robot can be
developed to improve machining processes. Here, particular
attention must be paid to the real-time requirements of the calculations and the associated dead time of the control system.

can be compensated for by the control system. The resulting
increased accuracy of the robot benefits every application. If,
in addition to the internal friction, compensation of the external



forces caused by the machining process is implemented in
the friction model, simulative experiments can be carried out.
This makes it possible to optimize the machining parameters
and identify critical sub-processes before the start of the
real process. Once a digital twin has been set up with its
communication and data storage components, it serves as a
central data memory. It can therefore be easily supplemented
by other applications, thus accelerating their development
process. In the present application case, for example, the
cutting parameters can be optimized to reduce _CO_ 2 or a
product quality prediction can be developed based on the
production data.
